Using an async context manager with Python 3.7 and Tornado 5.0+ solves this nicely. The [async_generator](https://pypi.org/project/async_generator/) may be used to backport this to as low as 3.5.

```
import contextlib

class AsyncSession(queries.TornadoSession):

    @contextlib.asynccontextmanager
    async def query(self, *args, **kwargs):
        result = await super().query(*args, **kwargs)
        try:
            yield result
        finally:
            result.free()

session = AsyncSession(...)
async with session.query(sql) as result:
    print(result)
```

Hi Anselm,

Thanks for your patches!

# 1. Timeouts
Can you send this one in another pull request? I'll merge it first.

# 2. Async
We need a way to handle multiple DBus messages at once, that's obvious. However, I don't think that going async in this way is a good idea. We get multiple ways to do everything, and the faster one is the hideous-looking one. And the main pydbus's goal is to enable writing perfect code.

A better-looking solution would be to return futures from methods that want to be executed asynchronously. This way, we don't need to pass them any special arguments - they simply return a concurrent.future object, and we subscribe to it's result with add_done_callback.

However, the best solution would be to use greenlets/gevent - and run all the methods in new greenlets, so that developers would magically get the performance of async handlers without writing the hideous code.

I don't know how much work is needed to make pydbus gevent-compatible (which includes making all the client functions non-blocking (in the gevent's sense)), however it would be quite useful. It would also seamlessly enable using pydbus in WSGI applications served with gunicorn/uwsgi/any other python server relying on green threads.

If you're interested in doing some research in that field, and possibly in porting pydbus to gevent - great! If not - I guess I'll do it next month.

# 3. Polkit
I think that the decorator should call the user's function only when the sender is authorized to execute that method. When he isn't, it should automatically raise PolkitAuthorization.NotAuthorizedError("You are not authorized!").

95% of cases do not need to handle not being authorized in any special way. For the remaining 5% we can provide a helper function that checks authorization and returns its status.

Also, Polkit has a D-Bus API - https://www.freedesktop.org/software/polkit/docs/latest/eggdbus-interface-org.freedesktop.PolicyKit1.Authority.html#eggdbus-method-org.freedesktop.PolicyKit1.Authority.CheckAuthorization

As we're the D-Bus library, I think that it may be a better idea to use it directly, without the polkit library - as I don't think the library is providing anything more than the DBus API.

BTW CheckAuthorization supports identifying clients by their DBus names (subject-kind = system-bus-name), so we might want to use this instead of checking the PID of the sender.

Hey @skrud, thanks for the report. This looks to be a bug or quirk in the Zendesk API itself. After messing around a bit I've found that the following updates the ticket subject as you would expect:

```python
zenpy_client = Zenpy(**creds)
ta = zenpy_client.tickets.create(Ticket(subject="The subject", description="description"))

new_ticket = ta.ticket

print new_ticket.subject
new_ticket.raw_subject = "I am the new subject"
new_ticket.subject = "I am the new subject"

ta = zenpy_client.tickets.update(new_ticket)
updated_ticket = ta.ticket
print updated_ticket.subject
``` 

Alternatively, if you know the `id` this also works:

```python
zenpy_client.tickets.update(Ticket(id=2, subject='A new subject for the ticket'))
```

@pmac also suggested the key be a list of keys. For example:

```
required_config.add_option(
    ['access_key', 's3_access_key', 'root:common_s3_access_key'],
    doc='AWS S3 access key'
)
```

I'm inclined not to do that because I think there's a nicety to implying there's a primary key we're looking for vs. the alternative keys which could be there because of backwards compatibility or looking at shared spaces or other things. From a documentation perspective, I think this is better:

```
ComponentFoo

   configuration:
      access_key
         default: None
         alternative keys: s3_access_key, root:common_s3_access_key

         AWS S3 access key.
```

It suggests which key to use so the user isn't like, "ok--I have 4 choices. Which one do I pick?" We could suggest developers deal with that in the `doc`, too, but I think there's a nicety here to address it by default.

Regarding using `default` for this, I think I'm inclined not to go that route. The problem here is that `default` already has "interesting behavior" and I don't think it helps to add to that. Having function values is interesting, though. Maybe that's something we can add later, but it definitely leads to a more complex documentation situation.

Pulling configuration from a shared space and supporting backwards compatibility with older configuration seem like important things to support and worth adding another argument.

Of the various options, I think I like `alternate_keys` best. I wish it were shorter, but I think it reads the best. I'm going to go with that for now. This is an alpha-quality library, so experimenting now is ok.

Thanks for the reminder, I just added a fix. This issue can happen because a sorted BAM is always sorted by the order of sequences in the header. But if those sequences are not themselves sorted, the resulting bedGraph will not be sorted. Here's a problematic sorted BAM file:

```
@HD     VN:1.3  SO:coordinate
@SQ     SN:chrX         LN:20000000
@SQ     SN:chr2L        LN:23011544
None    0       chrX    11      255     5M      *       0       0       CGACA   IIIII   NM:i:0  NH:i:1
None    16      chrX    71      255     5M      *       0       0       TTCTC   IIIII   NM:i:0  NH:i:1
None    16      chrX    141     255     5M      *       0       0       CACCA   IIIII   NM:i:0  NH:i:1
None    0       chr2L   71      255     5M      *       0       0       GAGAA   IIIII   NM:i:0  NH:i:1
None    0       chr2L   141     255     5M      *       0       0       TGGTG   IIIII   NM:i:0  NH:i:1
None    16      chr2L   151     255     5M      *       0       0       GTTCA   IIIII   NM:i:0  NH:i:1
None    0       chr2L   161     255     5M      *       0       0       GATAA   IIIII   NM:i:0  NH:i:1
None    0       chr2L   211     255     5M      *       0       0       AAATA   IIIII   NM:i:0  NH:i:1
```

In this fix in fcc03db, I'm checking for the text "bedSort" in stderr from bedGraphToBigWig. If it's there, re-sort the bedGraph and try again. I suspect that in most cases `samtools sort` on a BAM with a fixed header will be faster than `bedtools sort` on a large bedGraph, but this should be fine for one-off cases.

Yeah, our application `defersToThread` a lot.
I think another part of our problem was that these blocked crochet tasks were starving twisted's deferred-to-thread DNS lookups, which was then timing out and breaking the async stuff.
Perhaps separating crochet's threadpool from twisted's threadpool might be an option for us, but we'd still have a problem if too many threads were blocking on their own threadpool.

Our current patches on the ThreadPool just fix the case where the pool would be fully blocked in this way, because then it has no chance of making progress.
We don't dynamically adjust it in any other situation, and that seems to be working okay for our workloads.

In theory you could have an arbitrarily long loop of threads blocked on threadpools, but then you probably have bigger problems in your application ;)
Fixing the simplest case of threads blocking against work their containing threadpool was enough.

Please don't copy and paste code around from the example sources. They serve as an example to make you understand how to structure your own code, not as a direct implementation resource.

The command linked does not serve the purpose you were originally trying to in your own code. Your original issue is that you assigning the `discord.Server` dataclass to your `server` variable when you actually want an instance.

Instances relating to the current context can be found from the `Context` instance that is optionally passed to commands. An example of a command passing context is shown in the example [here.](https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py/blob/async/examples/basic_bot.py#L52)

From the **[How do I get the original message?](http://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/faq.html#how-do-i-get-the-original-message)** FAQ entry, we know that when we pass context, we can use `ctx.message` to get the original message.
Since this message is tied to the server, it has the [Message.server](https://discordpy.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api.html#discord.Message.server) attribute, so the Server in this context can be found through `ctx.message.server`.

Connecting these pieces together, the solution to your problem is to:
- Pass context using the keyword argument in the decorator (see the example)
- Use `ctx.message.server` to get the Server instance, rather than attempting to access the dataclass.

http://helga.readthedocs.org

Some initial requirements for this feature:
1. **Authentication** layer **must be** separated from **authorization** layer
2. Authentication layer is the only responsible for identifying clients and providing user object to the request context (the `req.context` dictionary). It should not have any other responsibilities.
3. Authorization layer is the only responsible for accessing/denying request to resources depending on user object from the context. It always relies on authentication layer.
4. Authentication should be global for the application (preferably as falcon middleware class) and it should be possible to provide multiple independent authentication layers. Order of processing authentication methods should be strictly defined, deterministic, and simple.
5. Authorization methods/requirements should be defined per-resource. Preferably using hooks.

but instead of picking the color randomly you could use the fontsize that is passed. For some reason I didn't pass the frequency to the color_func, but the fontsize should be a reasonable surrogate.
You need to map that to a float to use the color map, the easiest way I can come up with without thinking about it is just passing a max.

So something like
```python
class colormap_color_func(object):
    """Color func created from matplotlib colormap.
    Parameters
    ----------
    colormap : string or matplotlib colormap
        Colormap to sample from
    Example
    -------
    >>> WordCloud(color_func=colormap_color_func("magma"))
    """
    def __init__(self, colormap, max_font_size):
        import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
        self.colormap = plt.cm.get_cmap(colormap)
        self.max_font_size = max_font_size

    def __call__(self, word, font_size, position, orientation,
                 random_state=None, **kwargs):
        if random_state is None:
            random_state = Random()
        r, g, b, _ = 255 * np.array(self.colormap(font_size / self.max_font_size))
return "rgb({:.0f}, {:.0f}, {:.0f})".format(r, g, b)

What version of sqlservice are you using (the line numbers in the traceback seem to be for an older version)?

It seems like what might have happen is that you lost the connection while this session was checked out and then tried to use the session and got the invalid request error. I'm curious if the try/except in `sqlservice.core:transaction` ended up calling `rollback()` which then re-raised the exception. You'd likely be able to use the connection after the error but obviously handling prior to this would be better.

Was this a part of a long running process by chance? If so, you might be keeping the connection/session open and not properly closing it. If you can provide some more details/context of what's happening in this process, I might be able to point you in the right direction with more recommendations (e.g. if using MySQL/MariaDB need to ensure pool timeout in client are less than wait timeout in database config).

Another option is to use the `SQL_POOL_PRE_PING` config option to `SQLClient` (sqlservice >= 0.15.0) to tell SQLAlchemy to ping the connection when checking it out (http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/core/pooling.html#disconnect-handling-pessimistic). This does add some overhead when checking out a connection but it's more robust in that SQLAlchemy will invalidate the connection for you and attempt to reconnect when the ping fails. Things can still go wrong after the connection is checked out but rolling back and reconnecting should fix it. Usually SQLAlchemy will handle all this for you when you try to start the next db session (i.e. after you rollback, if the connection was invalidated, then SQLAlchemy will reconnect under the hood). So you just need to handle the failed transaction. But if the database is still down after the failed transaction, then you might want to try to detect that and handle that state appropriately.

Hi there, came to report this same issue—since it's already here, I have some further thoughts on a general-purpose solution to this problem.

> Solution: the next parameter should be filtered such that no domain/http/protocol is included.

I believe Flask's `url_for` (which is used to populate `next` in a normal scenario) generates a URL with protocol/hostname/port equal to the current request's (or there is at least some configuration where that's true). You'd have to `urllib.parse` it, validate it, and strip these parts before passing to `redirect()`, which seems inelegant.

Another issue might be that, in some cases, you intentionally redirect to a known different domain (e.g. Flask app that works over several subdomain, or you make use of a remote login API like OAuth to access your app's accounts).

While looking at potential solutions to this issue that can work with safe absolute URLs, I came across this snippet that seems reasonable (written for py2—in py3, `urlparse` module is `urllib.parse`): http://flask.pocoo.org/snippets/62/

It checks that the protocol is http or https, and that the hostname for the request and `next` redirect are the same (maybe it would be better to check the port too?). I personally wrapped `werkzeug.utils.redirect` in a custom method and call this instead of `redirect` when using potentially unsafe URLs:

    def safe_redirect(location, *args, **kwargs):
        response = unsafe_redirect(location, *args, **kwargs)
        if not response.headers.get('Location', None) or is_safe_url(response.headers.get('Location')):
            return response
        else:
            raise ValueError("Unsafe redirect blocked")

This seems pretty reasonable in general, but in some cases an app might need to use a different domain redirect (if the app handles subdomains with a common login, or is e.g. an OAuth login that redirects to a third-party site). Being able to specify a custom redirect filter might be useful in this case, for flask_user default views.

I might suggest as a solution for the flask_user views:

a) accept an `is_url_safe` callable in the UserManager constructor (with all the appropriate security warnings in documentation);
b) use a default `is_url_safe` implementation like above;
c) in flask_user, use a wrapper around `redirect()` to make sure that the `next` parameter is safe by checking `is_url_safe` (or by checking the Location header of the Response set by `redirect` if preferred, as it does do some conversion/normalisation—I haven't looked too deeply at this). (Maybe you can make this safe_redirect available in the API as `flask_user.safe_redirect` or something too, for any custom views users may write that use this `next` pattern.)

If you set `attr='my_attribute_name'` on your `LinkCol`, it should use the value from that attribute as the text for the link. It uses the name of the column as a fallback. Unlike with other columns, it does not borrow the attribute that you give the column in the table class to get the attribute from the item for the td.

[This example](https://github.com/plumdog/flask_table/blob/master/examples/simple_app.py) might be useful. In particular, if we removed `attr='name'` from the `LinkCol` in that example, we would just get name of the column, not the name attribute from the item. But we don't need to do this for the description field.

No, `Database` creates an engine then immediately asks that engine for a connection:

```python
        self._engine = create_engine(self.db_url, **kwargs)

        # Connect to the database.
        self.db = self._engine.connect()
```

Later, `Database.close` returns that connection to the pool. This means that the engine held by `Database` has a connection open for as long as it lives, even after calling `close`.

Connection pooling doesn't help since within the lifecycle of `Database`, one connection is checked out, then later returned.

I've been investigating this bug and find, that watchmedo is working correctly. The problem is that some editors don't fire filesystem events when file is modified and saved. Netbeans (7.0 and 7.1) or vim belongs to group of such editors. So when you create new file in this particular editor, it fires event notifying file creation, but when you modify it, watchmedo doesn't catch anything.

Dirty workaround is to omit --patterns switch. That way watchmedo will be listening on directory events, which are fired everytime included file changes. But there is a serious problem with this solution - 1 file change can fire N of directory events. So for example you compiler get started unnecessary N times. It would be nice to have option that group such directory events.
I hope some workaround I googled will work for you https://stackoverflow.com/a/7431385/358458

Check Stack-Overflow [Here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/31059477/sqlalchemy-unique-constraint-failed-error-yet-no-unique-constraints-set)

It says that : 
"Drop your tables or delete your DB and initialize it again. Every time you make a schema change you must drop your tables and re-initialize, it doesn't just update."

from miio import Vacuum
vac = Vacuum('192.168.1.2', 'token')
info = vac.info()

print("Signal: %s" % rssi)
```
(for a more generic solution, `Vacuum` can be replaced with `Device`)

I had similar problem and was able to fix it.
The key to understand what is causing that issue is that `init_social` method must be called only once per whole test session - if not, the column `social_auth_usersocialauth.uid` will be initialized over and over and causing this bug to appear. The simplest solution is just to create `app` once per whole test session. In case of the @benregn code change `app` fixture scope from "function" to "session".

Binning is perhaps the most common and straightforward solution. Just be careful, your target function is now a "step function" along the direction of the binned variable, meaning it's first derivative is ill defined at some points, making GP's life harder (specially with using a smooth kernel such as RBF). One way to mitigate this is by increasing the parameter `alpha` (see sklearn's GP documentation), allowing the process more flexibility when modelling the binned variable.

### tl;dr - All you need to do is to swap the quantities and the trade types.

### Longer explanation:
Let's say that our base market is `ETH` and that a `Trade` object is 
```python
{
  "symbol": "ABC",
  "type": "buy", # _(or "sell")_
  "quantity": decimal.Decimal('15.4'), # IMPORTANT! Minimum for USDT trades is 10
  "price": decimal.Decimal('<some irrelevant price because we operate @market>')
}
```

Usually, for regular tokens that are paired with `ETH` (e.g 'GVT' which would be designated as `GVTETH`) The following execution passes without any issues:
```python
symbol_pair = '{symbol}ETH'.format(symbol=trade['symbol']) # 'GVTETH'
client.order_market_buy(symbol=symbol_pair, quantity=trade['quantity'])
```
The problem as we saw is with the `USDT` market.

The reason for this is that the market is actually `ETHUSDT` (and not `USDTETH` like the regular tokens). Now, changing the market name is not enough, you actually operate on a `USDT` market and must flip the trade type AND the trade quantity to represent quantities of `ETH`

So if we want to sell 10.5 `USDT` to `ETH` and keep the "normal" structure we usually operate with (like the regular `ETH` paired tokens), we would have a trade which looks like this:
```python
{
   'type': 'sell',
   'symbol': 'USDT',
   'price': Decimal('1.0'), # again, not relevant here
   'quantity': Decimal('10.5')
}
``` 

And the code that would be executed correctly is:
_(the `usd_eth_price` is well.. the current USD price of `ETH`. One can find it using the `get_all_tickers()` and looking for the `ETHUSDT` symbol)_

```python
symbol_pair = 'ETH{symbol}'.format(symbol=trade['symbol']) # 'ETHUSDT'
flipped_quantity = round(trade['quantity'] / usd_eth_price, 3) # this can be more precise using the LOT_SIZE filter. For this simplified example it just works.
if trade['type'] == 'sell': # Flipping types of trades
  client.order_market_buy(symbol=symbol_pair, quantity=flipped_quantity)
else:
  client.order_market_sell(symbol=symbol_pair, quantity=flipped_quantity)
```

I've tested it multiple times with multiple quantities in both directions and it worked well for me.

I ran into this problem also. I debugged the issue a little bit and I think the problem is using the Django's postgresql_psycopg2.base.DatabaseWrapper's is_usable() method.

If there is an error in the query a rollback is to be performed. Django-postgrespool's rollback method uses DatabaseWrapper class' is_usable() method to check if the connection is usable and the rollback can be performed. How ever, the is_usable() method is internally using "SELECT 1" to check if the connection is usable but after the query error "SELECT 1" cannot be executed (postgres prints "ERROR:  current transaction is aborted, commands ignored until end of transaction block"), so in this case is_usable() always returns False. And that's the reason the django-postgrespool's rollback is never executed either, and autocommit is tried to set True even when the connection is actually still in a transaction.

This can be tested by removing is_usable() check from _rollback method in django-postgrespool's DatabaseWrapper class. After that you see the real error message.

To fix this we would need to change the way rollback is checking database connection status. Either by overriding the is_usable() method or changing rollback to use some other style.

Example for anyone else that may want a similar solution.
``` python
from functools import wraps
def phonenumber_memoizer(e164_prefix_len=5):
    """e164_prefix_len should be long enough to uniquely identify region information
    
    e164_prefix_len == 5 # +1NPA should be enough to identify a country
    e164_prefix_len == 8 # +1NPANXX should be enough to identify a description/city
    """
    def decorator(f):
        memo = {}
        @wraps(f)
        def wrapper(numobj, *args, **kwargs):
            #print("country_for_number_memoizer: country_code: {}".format(numobj.country_code))
            if numobj.country_code == 1:
                e164_num = format_number(numobj, phonenumbers.PhoneNumberFormat.E164) 
                npa = e164_num[:e164_prefix_len]
                if npa not in memo:
                    memo[npa] = f(numobj, *args, **kwargs)
                return memo[npa]
            else:
                return f(numobj, *args **kwargs)
        return wrapper
    return decorator

memo_number_type = phonenumber_memoizer(e164_prefix_len=8)(number_type)
memo_region_code_for_number = phonenumber_memoizer(e164_prefix_len=5)(region_code_for_number)
memo_country_name_for_number = phonenumber_memoizer(e164_prefix_len=5)(country_name_for_number)
memo_description_for_number = phonenumber_memoizer(e164_prefix_len=8)(description_for_number)

# setup p for test
p = parse('+13035551212', 'US')

print("parse_number:", timeit(lambda: parse('+13035551212','US'), number=10000))
print("format_number:", timeit(lambda: format_number(p, phonenumbers.PhoneNumberFormat.E164), number=10000))
print("is_possible_number:", timeit(lambda: is_possible_number(p), number=10000))
print("is_possible_number_with_reason:", timeit(lambda: is_possible_number_with_reason(p), number=10000))
print("is_valid_number:", timeit(lambda: is_valid_number(p), number=10000))
print
print("number_type:", timeit(lambda: number_type(p), number=10000))
print("region_code_for_number:", timeit(lambda: region_code_for_number(p), number=10000))
print("country_name_for_number:", timeit(lambda: country_name_for_number(p, 'en'), number=10000))
print("description_for_number:", timeit(lambda: description_for_number(p, 'en'), number=10000))
print
print("memo_number_type:", timeit(lambda: memo_number_type(p), number=10000))
print("memo_region_code_for_number:", timeit(lambda: memo_region_code_for_number(p), number=10000))
print("memo_country_name_for_number:", timeit(lambda: memo_country_name_for_number(p, 'en'), number=10000))
print("memo_description_for_number:", timeit(lambda: memo_description_for_number(p, 'en'), number=10000))
```

But if your content item provides the interface, why don't you just declare this and adapt it? No behavior or other extra magic needed.

```
>>> from zope.interface import implementer
>>> from plone.app.contenttypes.interfaces import ICollection
>>> from plone.dexterity.content import Item
>>> @implementer(ICollection)
... class MyCollection(Item):
...     pass
... 
>>> mycollection = MyCollection()
>>> ICollection(mycollection) is mycollection
True
```

Whole index wrapper class:
```
import hashlib
import json
import os
import time
from collections import OrderedDict, Iterable

from annoy import AnnoyIndex

import settings
from utils.misc import get_state


class AnnoyModel(object):
    """Wrapper-class for AnnoyIndex for convenient loading/saving and encapsulation
    of {real_item_id: ordinal_number} mapping """

    NUM_TREES = settings.ANNOY_NUM_TREES
    METRIC = settings.ANNOY_METRIC

    def __init__(self, num_trees=NUM_TREES, metric=METRIC, model=None, ids=None, dim=None, fitted_at=None):
        self._model = model
        self._ids = ids
        self.dim = dim

        self.num_trees = num_trees
        self.metric = metric
        self.fitted_at = fitted_at

    @property
    def model(self):
        """AnnoyIndex"""
        if self._model is None:
            raise Exception("Model had not been built")
        return self._model

    @property
    def ids(self):
        """OrderedDict like {real_id: ordinal_id}"""
        if self._ids is None:
            raise Exception("No id-ordinal mapping")
        return self._ids

    def build(self, features):
        """
        Builds AnnoyIndex and mappings
        Args:
            features: list of (id, feature_vector) tuples
            num_trees: number of trees in AnnoyIndex
            metric: type of metric to use in Annoy

        Returns:

        """
        if not isinstance(features, Iterable):
            raise TypeError("Wrong type of features parameter, expected Iterable, got {}".format(type(features)))

        dim = len(features[0][1])
        ids = OrderedDict()
        model = AnnoyIndex(dim, self.metric)

        for i, (id_, vector) in enumerate(features):
            if isinstance(vector, (list, tuple)):
                raise TypeError("Bad type of feature vector")

            if len(vector) != dim:
                raise ValueError("Inconsistent sizes of feature vectors")

            model.add_item(len(ids), vector)
            ids[id_] = i

        was_built = model.build(self.num_trees)

        if not was_built:
            raise Exception("Cannot build AnnoyIndex")

        self._model = model
        self._ids = ids
        self.dim = dim
        self.fitted_at = int(time.time())

    def get_nns_by_id(self, id_, n, search_k=-1, include_distances=False):
        """
        Return N nearest neighbours by ID of item
        Args:
            id_: id of an item
            n: number of nearest neighbours
            search_k: depth of search, -1 search in all trees
            include_distances: whether return distances

        Returns: list of ids or tuple of list of ids and list of distances

        """
        result = self.model.get_nns_by_item(self.ids[id_], n, search_k, include_distances)

        if include_distances:
            return [(self.ids.keys()[r]) for r in result[0]], result[1]
        return [self.ids.keys()[r] for r in result]

    def get_nns_by_vector(self, vector, n, search_k=-1, include_distances=False):
        """
        Return N nearest neighbours by feature vector
        Args:
            vector: list of float
            n: number of neighbours
            search_k: depth of search, -1 search in all trees
            include_distances: whether return distances

        Returns: list of ids or tuple of list of ids and list of distances

        """
        result = self.model.get_nns_by_vector(vector, n, search_k, include_distances)

        if include_distances:
            return [(self.ids.keys()[r]) for r in result[0]], result[1]
        return [self.ids.keys()[r] for r in result]

    def get_item_vector(self, id_):
        """
        Features of item
        Args:
            id_: int id of item

        Returns: list of floats

        """
        return self.model.get_item_vector(self.ids[id_])

    def get_distance(self, i, j):
        """
        Return the distance between items
        :param i: id of first item
        :param j: id of second item
        """
        return self.model.get_distance(self.ids[i], self.ids[j])

    def add_item(self, id_, vector):
        """
        Adds item to index
        Args:
            id_: id of item
            vector: feature vector

        Returns:

        """
        self.model.add_item(len(self.ids), vector)
        self.ids[id_] = len(self.ids)

    @property
    def state(self):
        """ Current serializable state of the model
        """
        return get_state(
            self,
            invalid_types=(AnnoyIndex,)
        )

    @property
    def name(self):
        """ Model save path without ts
        """
        state = self.state
        return "{cls}_{params}_{fitted_at}".format(
            cls=state["class"],
            params=hashlib.md5(json.dumps([
                state["dim"], state["num_trees"], state["metric"], state["_ids"]
            ], sort_keys=True)).hexdigest(),
            fitted_at=state["fitted_at"]
        )

    def save(self, base_path):
        """
        Saves model with meta on disk
        Args:
            base_path: base path for models

        Returns:

        """
        path = os.path.join(base_path, self.name)
        if not os.path.exists(path):
            os.makedirs(path)
        if not self.model.save(os.path.join(path, "index")):
            raise Exception("Cannot save AnnoyIndex")

        with open(os.path.join(path, "meta"), "w") as f:
            f.write(json.dumps(self.state))

    @classmethod
    def load(cls, path):
        """
        Loads model with meta from disk
        Args:
            path: absolute path of model

        Returns: AnnoyModel

        """
        with open(os.path.join(path, "meta")) as f:
            state = json.loads(f.read())

        ids = [(int(k), int(v)) for k, v in state["_ids"].iteritems()]
        ids = OrderedDict(sorted(ids, key=lambda x: x[1]))
        dim = state["dim"]
        model = AnnoyIndex(dim)

        if not model.load(os.path.join(path, "index")):
            raise Exception("Cannot load AnnoyIndex")

        return cls(ids=ids, dim=dim, model=model, fitted_at=state["fitted_at"], num_trees=state["num_trees"],
                   metric=state["metric"])

```

Hehe ok just to explain what I mean: one classical example is: if you have two classes `Square` and `Rectangle` – which one inherits from which? `Square` is a special instance of `Rectangle` but you could also argue that `Rectangle` extends `Square`. So both ways kinda makes sense

The distinction is not in the parameter level, it's on the parameter *value* level. In the example above you can use both ways. To be clear let's say the code is this

```python
class SomeTask(luigi.Task):
   par = luigi.Parameter(default=456)
```

`python script.py SomeTask --par 123` will run a `SomeTask(par=123)`. If some other task in the chain instantiates `SomeTask()` then it will instantiate it with `SomeTask(par=456)`.
`python script.py SomeTask --SomeTask-par 123` will run a `SomeTask(par=123)`, but will also set the value on a class level to 123. This means if some other task in the chain instantiates `SomeTask()` then it will be created with `SomeTask(par=456)`.

The class level values are meant mostly for config. I.e. if you have a lot of Hadoop jobs that inherit from a common class, then the idea is you can specify `HadoopJobTask--queue my-queue` to set the right queue. Things like that.

It comes down to eventual consistency as described [here](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/APIReference/query-api-troubleshooting.html#eventual-consistency). So essentially, you may have to wait a bit to have the security group available to you. For this reason, we expose a waiter interface that will poll till a resource reaches a particular state instead of sleeping for an arbitrary set of time. [Here](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/ec2.html#waiters) are the waiters available for EC2 currently. It looks like we do not have a waiter for security groups, but that is something we could add as it looks like you have a valid use case for it. Let us know what you think.

Hey @memolipd once you do an execute on a payment, you can [look up the payment](https://developer.paypal.com/docs/api/#look-up-a-payment-resource). The related_resources section has the sale item which has an id, payment.transactions[0].related_resources[0].sale.id. You can use that id to fetch the status of that sale ([sample](https://github.com/paypal/PayPal-Python-SDK/blob/master/samples/sale/find.py)) instead of having to look up the payment resource.

Pipfiles are the source of truth for a pipenv managed virtualenv.  The minute your pipfile has changed, you need to re-resolve dependencies.

Hashes of the pipfile are already tracked, and we are tracking the overall performance concerns in a few other places, but it is not exactly straightforward to handle.  Just because the lockfile indicates that something was installed doesn't mean it actually _is_ installed, so whenever we run an installation we let pip confirm that the packages specified are actually installed.
It would be best to have a test for the `isdir` change, but redundant tests are not a problem.

@jaraco One simple solution that I kind of like, to avoid having to defer the search until `namespace_packages` is known, would be to change the behavior of `include` such that a directory in `include` is considered a package despite lacking `__init__.py`, and then instruct users to add their PEP 420 packages to `include`.

So it would be something like

```python
implicit_namespace_packages=[...]

setup(
    ...
    packages=find_packages(include=implicit_namespace_packages, ...)
    namespace_packages=[...] + implicit_namespace_packages
)
```

I know it requires the user to introduce a variable to avoid having to type the list of implicit namespace packages twice, but I could live with that. For many use cases (e.g. a "suite" of distributions) I think the list of PEP 420 packages should be short (often just a single one), and the user might then even skip the variable.

I think it makes sense that if we've now discovered that `find_packages` needs more info to do its job, that the info must be passed in as a parameter.

I'm reluctant to expand the exposed API of virtualenv. Longer term, I believe people should be moving to the stdlib venv and virtualenv should either disappear (except to support Python 2.x) or become a thin wrapper around venv. To that end, offering any APIs that venv doesn't provide will make that harder.

Surely the canonical way to get site-packages is `sysconfig.get_path('purelib')` (or platlib...)? I know it involves invoking the target interpreter, but that seems a reasonable constraint to me.

It's conceivable that there is a need for a library to offer a way to introspect aspects of a Python interpreter, *without* running that interpreter. It's even conceivable that virtualenv might benefit from using such a library. But I don't think we have the resources to maintain such a library as part of virtualenv (especially given the myriad of special cases that exist - Debian's dist-packages, this PyPI variation, Windows vs Unix bin/scripts distinction, etc).

Well, what we have for first pair's value is an empty node. From YAML spec:

> YAML allows the node content to be omitted in many cases. Nodes with empty content are interpreted as if they were plain scalars with an empty value. Such nodes are commonly resolved to a “null” value.

So null is resolved value. For canonical representation it's an empty string, since there're no nulls there. AFAIK it's not possible to disable null "resolving" in JS-YAML, but this isn't a practical use-case, and JS-YAML is all about practical use.

I strongly favor the first option, but it isn't a simple one. The second requires a fuzzy range around the true, valid range which is magically assumed to be incorrect and then gets clipped. This may not be the case, thus our magic would be incorrect.

A `@clip_if_input_valid` decorator would be ideal. However, it could only be applied to functions where it can be proven that for valid inputs, valid output will be obtained every time. And our "anything in, anything out" style makes this a challenge, as the output dtype needs to be inspected before clipping gets applied. Here are a couple issues off the top of my head:

* Histogram/contrast adjustment of a valid uint8 image. Valid input means the decorator thinks it should clip, but the output _should_ be floating point, retaining the input's range, to enable maximum fidelity in a workflow. It incorrectly gets clipped to 1.0.
* Valid image passed to `util.random_noise` with `clip=False` set deliberately, so the random noise input retains its full range. Instead, the decorator clips it.

Also worth noting the solution can't be overly complicated or burdensome, as this would be yet another thing every new contributor/maintainer needs to learn and understand.

Here are measures that can taken to mitigate delays for zopefoundation builds

1. `sudo` isn't actually necessary anymore
2. we can use `allow_failures` on the manylinux builds along with `fast_finish` so that, per build, providing feedback developers is no slower. This would have the effect of turning the build green as soon as the current builds for linux and mac os x are done. However, the manylinux builds would still be run, which means more builds in zopefoundation queue
3. We quickly bail out of the manylinux build **except** on tagged commits. Since we only want manylinux builds in order to make manylinux wheels, this could work well. If we felt confident about mac os x, we could do the same for those builds. This would both mean a minimal increase in per-build feedback (for not tagged commits), but also a minimal contribution to queue congestion.

If you have a kernel already, you can do `kaggle kernels pull -k {username}/{kernel-id} -p /path/to/download --metadata`  to pull the code down, and `kaggle kernels push -p /path/to/kernel` to push new code once you've made changes.

The problem here is that all python modules are global, and it's likely psutil would have already been installed by something else. For example, it's a mandatory dependency on ubuntu (cannot be removed) and ships by default with many windows python distributions including activestate (cannot be removed). Ubuntu 10.04 for example ships with psutil 1.2.1. If we add a version requirement on such a system, setup.py will fail: `pkg_resources.VersionConflict: (psutil 1.2.1 (/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages), Requirement.parse('psutil>=5.2.0'))` But there's no way for the user to fix this because the version that comes on their system cannot be replaced or upgraded in many cases. --- *Comments from [Reviewable](https://reviewable.io:443/reviews/google/mobly/161)*

Hi Dom-nik,
  it's caused by spark launching new executor after the initialisation of H2OContext.

In Sparkling Water we try to discover all spark executors at the start of H2OContext and start h2o on them. But if spark for some reason launches new executor, it does not have h2o instance running which then leads to error during computation.

So what we do in this case is to throw exception on spark topology changes and kill the cloud.
You can turn this listener off by setting `spark.ext.h2o.topology.change.listener.enabled` to `false`, but it it's still won't prevent from the problem I described earlier ( it's also explained here https://github.com/h2oai/sparkling-water/issues/4)

We are working on a new sparkling-water architecture which should solve these issues.

Right now Patroni does not support synchronous replication: particularly, what needs to be implemented is setting the application_name/syncrhonous_standby_name and making sure the we go into the synchronous mode only after we bootstrap at least one replica. As for selecting the sync replica by default as a target for the failover, it is not necessary: Patroni will select the replica that is the closest to the master by the position in the WAL recently replayed.

Looks good. I think it makes sense to enrich the history further (perhaps in subsequent PRs), for instance, adding the current leader name to the leader_timeline - 1. I'm not sure I agree with the idea of having a separate replica timeline cache in addition to the cached information stored in the cluster_info_state, why not use the cluster_info_state['cached_timeline'] for that, or even revert back to the idea of having the 'leader' status separate from the timeline, as checking for the timeline = 0 instead of leader = 'f' is potentially more error-prone. I must admit I don't know why the detail::f methods are calling add_function(const T&) instead of add_function(initializer_list). In both cases, it would be good to ensure detail::f calls add_function(initializer_list) so we can tell there are inputs and set the device appropriately.

Thanks so much for support, but even in the postscript hintset case this is semantically incomplete: the given pointTag labels depend on contour order and their respective starting points.

When anything changes, the label will survive but the hint replacement will have to be regenerated, most likely resulting in different hint sets and pointTag labels to become valid again. And the information needed to do so isn’t encoded in the autohinter output. ‘Links’ would be more useful than raw hint positions and widths.

For higher level hinting commands as in VTTtalk we would like to see point tag references to source point indices and to target points that become touched (in horizontal or vertical direction), and the ascii strings that make up the actual command then need to be marked to make clear which arguments are indices… possible but not simple. And perhaps better suited to a less generic representation…

@Lukasa,

Here is an update (because I never feel confortable not understanding things 100%, and went digging deeper):
* This PR does improve connection reuse during the authentication process.
* The `response.raw.release_conn()` above seems "by-the-book" in the sense that it returns the kept-alive connection to the pool, which (apparently) must be done because there is a very loose coupling between HTTPAdapter (used to send subsequent requests) and the underlying PoolManager.
* This PR does not, however, ensure two fully independent app-level requests (two threads, for example) are NTLM-authenticated correctly: not unless the pools managed by the PoolManager use block=True and hold a maximum of one conncection. Again, this stems from the loose coupling between HTTPAdapater and the PoolManager: on send, the HTTPAdapater does `conn = self.get_connection(..)` which, in turn, ends up calling PoolManager's `connection_from_url(..)`.

More:
* I raised the parallelism in my use case and quickly found failures with this PR, with PR #51 with v0.1.0;  with either the default PoolManager or the variation I shared in #56.
* For some reason, using a PoolManager with block=1 and a forcing its underlying pools to have at most one connection, lead me to some hangs which I haven't diagnosed yet (but then again, by code base is somewhat complex).

Summary:
* I'd like to understand this even better (in particular, why I'm getting hangs -- maybe I'll write a simpler test client that reproduces the failures I'm observing and go from there).
* If anyone can chime in with an idea or point out where I'm mistaken, that'd be great.
* Nonetheless in the single-threaded case (and in lots of other threaded cases, but not all) this PR does indeed improve connection reuse.

Again,
Thanks in advance for any input.

To your points:

1. I converted `MetricDescriptor`, `ResourceDescriptor`, and `LabelDescriptor` into ordinary classes. This could well be helpful when we add support for defining new custom metrics.

   I left `Metric`, `Resource`, `TimeSeries`, and `Point` as named tuples. If there is a need to revisit them, it would be best to do it in the context of writing data to custom metrics.

   There are already a couple of named tuple classes of this sort in `streaming/http_wrapper.py`, but they are not exposed.

2. The code for listing metric descriptors and resource descriptors no longer uses nested functions (not that there is anything wrong with nested functions). It would not be improved by rewriting to use the `Iterator` base class.

   Moreover, it is better for these particular calls to return lists than iterators.

3. I don't have a preference as to whether to squash or not.

4. Test coverage is now at 100%. However, a number of the unit tests fail because `pandas` is not installed in the test environment. See #1710.

> `imageio.imread('checkerboard.png')`

I am still worried about intent here. The documentation says that `imageio.imread` can read from many "sources", but this particular snippet implicitly says that you are going to read a `.jpg` stored *somewhere* locally, and users would probably assume it would be under the current working directory.

However, what this snippet effectively does is look for the file in a special directory, **and**, if the file is not there, download it from what you believe is a *trusted* location. From a security standpoint, this could be raised as a concern. I believe you could (and should) keep the magic directory feature (provided it is documented), but drop or make explicit the auto-download part.

> How strongly do you feel about this?

I foresee the autownload feature will raise some eyebrows at the review stage of the package. That's why I prefer to be honest upfront and propose alternate solutions before promising anything.

> Uses manual SQL queries to delete from specific tables, rather than doing Pythonland cascade deletion.

This doesn't happen if you call `delete()` on a QuerySet. It happens if you call `delete()` on an object instance.

I am just reciting this out of memory, but also a distinct feeling that I've done it before :) If you don't mind, could you try and see if things are still working if you make this change?

```python
delete_related_template = """
    DELETE FROM {table}
        WHERE {fk_field} IN (
            SELECT id FROM {cn_table} WHERE tree_id = '{tree_id}'
        )
"""
```

Becomes:

```python
function delete_ids_by_fk_field_in_tree(DeleteFromModel, ChannelModel, fk_field, tree_id):
    # Construct the IN query
    kwargs={fk_field + "__in": ChannelModel.objects.filter(tree_id=tree_id).values('id')}

    # Construct and execute the DELETE query
    DeleteFromModel.filter(**kwargs).delete()
```

Hi Wayne,


Suppose i is the point you want to interpolate to. This is from C-points j that i is strongly connected to.

(Call this S_i.) As you noted, in doing this secondary approximation for e_k for k in F or weak connections

in the neighborhood of i, you have to look at connections from k to points in S_i, and this sum over all these

connections can be zero. The easiest way to handle this is to take this secondary sum only over points also

in S_k (which are presumably all the same sign). By the usual choice of the coarse grid, this set should be

nonempty, so the sum should be nonzero. (You could also take this sum over just all "right-sign" connections,

again keeping the denominator nonzero.)


John

Subject: Re: [pyamg/pyamg] Standard AMG interpolation, unknown approach for systems, coarse grid visualization (#182)

Jacob and Ben,
Ok, I've finally gotten around to looking at this again.
Using slices for the extract_diagonal_blocks() function was quite simple (I think when I wrote this originally, I didn't realize you could just use the same slicing syntax on the scipy sparse matrices).
As far as debugging standard interpolation, I see that for the 'bar' example, we get zeros for the inner_denominator in ruge_stuben.h. This means that when we sum over the connections between node k (a strongly connected fine point) and the other strongly connected C points, we get cancellation to zero.
This cannot happen if all off-diagonal elements are the same sign (as is the case for M matrices). The 'bar' example matrix doesn't have this nice property, however, so we can get cancellation.
I don't know whether there is a standard fix for this problem or not. I've included John on this email to get his input here. As I understand it, the idea is to account for the contribution from fine node k by spreading its contribution to the other connected C points. The inner denominator sum serves as part of a weighted averaging used when spreading node k's contribution to the other C points. With this in mind, it sort of makes sense to me that you could sum absolute values for the inner denominator (thus avoiding cancellation). I'm not sure you'd end up with the appropriate sign though, as I've not really thought much about the interpolation weight formula in a non- M matrix context...
Thoughts from yall on this? Let me know if it's not clear what I'm talking about. I'm sure John probably knows of some solution here.
Thanks,
Wayne

@matt-gardner Hello, I'm not familiar with how allennlp's tests are done, otherwise I would be happy to help out if you can give me some instructions to do?

It is well understood that training neural nets, especially deep neural nets, has some variance that is due only to the random seed they were trained with.  That variance can sometimes be surprisingly large. 
 See, e.g., [this paper](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Reporting-Score-Distributions-Makes-a-Difference-P-Reimers-Gurevych/0eae432f7edacb262f3434ecdb2af707b5b06481) for some recent discussion on that topic.

AllenNLP provides reference implementations of published models, where we do our best to mimic the computation performed by the reported model, and a set of trained weights that come as close as we can manage to the originally-reported performance.  However, the originally-reported performance very likely just took a max over a large number of samples from the training distribution, especially if the paper came from Google*, like the decomposable attention model did.  We also took the max over the training runs we did when picking a model to put on the website.  I haven't characterized the variance of the decomposable attention model yet (@DeNeutoy might have better insight there), but I know that BiDAF can get anywhere from low 67 EM to high 68 EM, almost two full points difference, depending on the random seed.

Anyone doing research with neural nets needs to be familiar with these issues, and should not expect to be able to reproduce the maximum performance that we saw in our training samples on their first training run.  That's one of the reasons _why_ we provide the max from the samples we've taken, so you don't have to spend your GPU time reproducing that result.  We _do_ need to do a better job explaining this on our website, though, and it's on our list of things to do.  We are just a very small team, and we haven't gotten to it yet.

\* No offense intended to Google, they just have a whole lot of compute available and do very large grid searches over hyper-parameters.  If the variance due to the random seed is larger than the effect the hyper-parameters have on performance, you're effectively just increasing the number of samples from your training distribution.

@Grindizer, I'm at a loss in terms of what's going on here.  @bacongobbler mentioned the difference in how things are named being a potential culprit.  I've checked up on those regexes and decided to double-check them.

TL;DR-- nothing wrong with the container names or the regular expressions that try to match them.

With a container named `k8s_rover-cmd.9005fbc7_rover-v8-cmd-z6wzs_rover_82f14b08-b7c2-11e5-946e-12d891e80f69_3919bd5f`, this line in the logspout code:

https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/logspout/logspout.go#L109

Should match it and extract the tokens:

* rover
* cmd-z6wzs

This line incorporates those into a log message:

https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/logspout/logspout.go#L87-L92

The format of that message will be:

```
<timestamp> rover[cmd-z6wzs]: <message>
```

On the logger end, the app name is extracted from the message using this regex:

https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/logger/syslogish/server.go#L21

Given a message formatted as indicated above and given that regular expression, it seems that `rover` is correctly identified as the application that the log message originated from.  It _should_ be slotted into the ringbuffer for that application.

fwiw, I use this little online tool for testing regular expressions:

https://regex-golang.appspot.com/assets/html/index.html

After thinking about this and discussing with coworkers I think you should consider the option to integrate LMOD for the use of these `{de,}activate.d` scripts.

Just out of the box it would support a good number of shells, quoting the [TACC page](https://www.tacc.utexas.edu/research-development/tacc-projects/lmod):
> All the popular shells are supported: bash, ksh, csh, tcsh, zsh. LMOD is also available for perl and python.

This list of supported shells is clearly missing Windows support but since Lua is supported on Windows it would just be a matter of investing the time to add Windows abilities to LMOD.

And then you could leave the option to create shell specific `{de,}activate.d}` scripts for the more exotic shells (e.g. xonsh, fish). But I'd assume that even these can be added.

Not only would this be a reasonably light weight way to address these scripts but LMOD could even be used for the conda activate/deactivate processes.

To get a new subshell with an activated environment, you can, e.g., with `bash` use
```bash
 bash --rcfile <(echo '. ~/.bashrc ; . /path/to/conda/bin/activate')
```
(or, of course, put `. ~/.bashrc ; . /path/to/conda/bin/activate` in a persistent `file` and use `bash --rcfile file`.)

yes, the limit is being ignored because `reindex` uses `scan` search to get all the documents, you'd have to craft a query that only matches N documents in your index.

But if you look into the implementation of `reindex` you see how easy it is, it is literally feeding search results into `bulk`, which you can do on your own with the limited `search` API call:

```
data = es_production.search(index='i', body={"size": 1000, "query": {...}})
bulk(es_sandbox, data['hits']['hits'])
```

The proper way would be to create a single instance and share it across threads - the underlying connection pooling (by `urllib3` by default) is thread safe so that way connections will be reused across threads.

I haven't looked at the botocore library, but I doubt very much that my implementation does anything different.  The algorithm is well documented and pretty simple and AWS has a site that even provides the example python code.  By leveraging your nice Connection/Transport framework, the implementation was quite easy and has no impact on the rest of the library.

The advantage, and it may be minor, to my solution is that it eliminates the dependency on botocore that you may not need installed.  I'm using elasticsearch-py with https://github.com/nbs-system/naxsi/tree/master/nxapi and that will be deployed into a Docker container.  In accordance with my general approach of minimizing as much as possible what goes into a Docker container, I want to minimize the dependencies as much as possible.  Botocore is one library, but it depends on a handful more, and each of those might depend on more.  I haven't done a full dependency analysis, so I'm not sure of the total transitive dependencies brought in by botocore.  Given the simplicity of adding it directly to elasticsearch-py, I thought it was easier to do that than worry about botocore.

I'm guessing that a lot of people who use elasticsearch-py are also using much of the rest of the AWS CLI, which means that dependency will already be installed.  For that use case, botocore will also already be installed.  In the case of my Docker container, however, it will only be running nxtools, and AWS cli will not be installed.

Thanks.

The fact that reserved arguments and field names would share a namespace in the signature of `Model.__init__` is a major caveat. This sort of thing is best left for application-specific implementations.

A few things.  

First off - when you define a function and then pass it too `grad`, any explicit input is treated as a variable to differentative (so in your case the inputs `ttype` and `const`).  Define those elswhere than the definition of `myfun` instead of having them be explicit inputs.

For example, below is a simplified version of your `myfun` without these inputs, and where I have used `ttype = square`.  

```
def myfun(x):
    return np.linalg.norm(x)*const**2
```

The second thing: before trying an outside optimizer try one of the standard ones in the `autograd` library itself located at https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/master/autograd/misc/optimizers.py  This will help make sure there's no problem with your code prior to trying i

For example, you can run a simple gradient descent procedure like below - which works fine on my machine.  

```
# define derivatives and constant
costgrd = grad(myfun)
costhes = hessian_vector_product(myfun)
const = 100.

# run simple gradient descent loop
x = np.random.rand(1, 5)
alpha = 0.1
for i in range(100):
    x = x - alpha*df(x)
```

Hi,

This is not a pytest-asyncio specific issue actually: pure `xUnit` test classes (`TestCase` subclasses) can't access pytest fixtures, because tests can't have any other parameter besides `self` in the `xUnit` framework.

You will have to convert the class to "pytest-style" test class instead:

```python
class Test:  # note that no subclassing is needed

    @pytest.mark.asyncio
    def test_normalize_button_value(self, event_loop):
        my_serial = EsploraSerial("/dev/ttyACM0", self.q, self.cond)
        resp = event_loop.run_until_complete(my_serial.normalize_button_value(self.list_test_data, 1))
        assert resp == '0'
```

Just to update this issue on what I did when integrating Tornado and confluent-kafka-python: 

1. I created a dedicated thread that calls `Producer().poll` infinitely. 
2. I attached a callback function to `Producer().produce` that adds another callback function [2a] to the Tornado event loop. See [IOLoop.add_callback](http://www.tornadoweb.org/en/stable/ioloop.html#tornado.ioloop.IOLoop.add_callback) 
2a. The second callback function sets the result of the future once the message is delivered to Kafka (i.e. `poll` triggers the callback function)
```
        if err is None:
            future.set_result(True)
        else:
            future.set_result(False)

       return future 
```

It all works fine, however, it becomes complicated if you try and send two non-blocking messages in different coroutines on the main thread. There is no guarantee that the order in which you send the messages will be the same order that the callback function is triggered. 

This means that I need to wait until each message is sent successfully before sending another. That flow results in an average latency around ~2ms per message (with` queue_buffering_max_ms=0`)  to a local Kafka cluster. That's roughly ~500 messages / second compared to 20,000 messages / second using the `produce` method without the coroutine.
 
I guess my next step would be to wait until this issue is resolved and set `max.inflight=1`:
- https://github.com/edenhill/librdkafka/issues/1092

Question: what is the best way to support two non-blocking requests coming from the same main thread given that the responses may be out-of-order?

Projecting a variant that overlaps a sequence alignment isn't yet supported. Unfortunately, we don't even warn about it. This is one of those cases.

It's related to #259 and #353. 

A (poor) workaround for the moment is to use the VariantMapper, which doesn't validate. You'll get this:

```
$ hgvs-shell
>>> var_g = hp.parse_hgvs_variant("NC_000002.11:g.73675227_73675228insCTC")
>>> var_c = vm.g_to_c(var_g, "NM_015120.4")
>>> str(var_c)
'NM_015120.4:c.1573_1577insCTC'
```

Note that the 27_28 genomic insert expanded to 73_77. That's because the variant is right at the junction of a 3nt insertion. As described (kinda) in the other issues is that the behavior here is ill-defined.  

Here's the alignment: 
```
>>> print(variant_context_w_alignment(am37, var_g, tx_ac="NM_015120.4", margin=10))
   NM_015120.4  c       1563 >                            >     1587 NM_015120.4:c.1577_1579dup
   NM_015120.4  n       1674 > GGCTGTAAGTTCTCCTCTAGAAACT  >     1698 NM_015120.4:n.1688_1690dup
                               |||||||||||   |||||||||||  11=3I11= 
   NC_000002.11 g   73675217 > GGCTGTAAGTT---CTCTAGAAACT  > 73675238 NC_000002.11:g.73675227_73675228insCTC
   NC_000002.11 g   73675217 < CCGACATTCAA---GAGATCTTTGA  < 73675238 NC_000002.11:g.73675227_73675228insCTC
                                         ><                NC_000002.11:g.73675227_73675228insCTC
```

Although this seems simple because your insertion is exactly the difference between the two sequences, the general solution to handling variants in the context of alignment gaps is tricky (or, at least to me it is).

Progress.  I grok the test now.  The code I submitted is following the pattern for `test_aoi_models`. It defines a `ModelChain` object, uses the `run_model` method and inspects the output.  I had an error that I was examining the `ac` output instead of the `spectral_modifier` output. Now I'm up against a design decision for the test.

When we test individual functions like the spectral models we have to know the expected output. So I made up values for `precipitable_water` and `airmass_absolute` and computed the values each spectral model should produce. However, we are testing these functions as part of a model chain, where the output of the function being tested depends on the output of upstream functions. In this case, the output of `sapm_spectral_loss` depends on `airmass` which is calculated using the `times` input, ignoring the made-up values for `precipitable_water` and `airmass_absolute` I supplied when the model chain is created in `test_spectral_models`.

I see three ways to resolve the problem, neither of which are appealing:
1) I recalculate the expected values by running each model in the chain.  I don't like this approach, because a future change to one of the upstream functions could cause this test of the spectral models to fail.
2) Change the test pattern to not use a `ModelChain` object.  This is a problem because it wouldn't use the `ModelChain` methods which assign the spectral model method to the `ModelChain` object.
3) Add code to `ModelChain.__init__` to handle `airmass` as a kwarg, and impose user-supplied values in place of the calculation using `times` and `location`. This is my least liked option because I don't see a use of the additional code beyond this made-up test.

What is preferred here?  I'll proceed with the first option, but perhaps there's a fourth option I don't see.

Admittedly my description and the questions above is rather complex and long-winded. I give it another try:

The problem
==========
(1) Given a base SMT-problem (CSP), which is guaranteed to be sat. Thus, when solving the base CSP, we obtain a solution (model), called base solution.

(2) Given a set of variable-value pairs. We wish to generate a set of extra solutions that comprise all variable-value pairs compatible with the base CSP. The cardinality of the set shall be minimized. The minimum does not have to be exact. Rather we are satisfied with an approximate minimum (in order to lower the time and effort for producing the extra solutions).

An algorithm (simpler than the one described above)
==========================================

Phase A: Weed out incompatible variable-value pairs
-------------------------------------------------------------------
(1) Compute a base solution and save it.

(2) For each variable-value pair: 
- push the context
- use assert to assign the value to the variable
- check-sat. 
- If unsat, discard the variable-value pair. 
- pop the context.

The set of remaining variable-value pairs are all compatible with the base CSP.

Phase B: Generate extra solutions
-------------------------------------------
Compute a base solution.
While there is an unused variable-value pair
- push the context
- add  to the base CSP all unused variable-value pairs via (assert (= var val))
- compute a solution that maximizes the number of variable-value pairs occuring in the solution and save it
- pop the context
- remove all variable-value pairs that occured in the solution

Basically each round in the while loop executes a max-sat algorithm with two sets of constraints:
- a base set of constraints that must be obeyed
- a second set of simple variable-value constraints of which a maximum number shall be obeyed

Remarks
------------
By splitting the algorithm into two phases, the algorithm for the second phase can be much simpler, and there are more possibilities for implementing it.

I suspect that a general weighted max-sat algorithm would work, if the weights assigned to the base constraints were sufficiently high.
@delcypher I think it avoids pushing/popping a context at it "implicitly" creates one. That's the user side of things of course.

The problem encoded in the SMT file does not assert that the result of the expression is less than 0, instead it asserts that the result is less than `((_ to_fp 11 53) RTZ 1.0 -127)`, which is not the same as 0. If we replace it with an actual 0 then Z3 reports unsat as expected, but if we assert that it must be less than even the smallest positive floating point number `(fp #b0 #b00000000000 #x0000000000001)` then there is the trivial solution a=b=c=0.

Bit-widths should be the same. You can zero-extend bits to have them aligned. 
There is no auto-alignment to second-guess user intent.
Exception messages are shared among all interfaces and uses the SMT2 syntax in this case.

The format is a*infinity +b + c*epsilon, where the first and last component vanish when a resp. c are 0.
So kludge 1 is to rely on this format.
Perhaps the feedback is basically that we need to redesign the format of the output. 
We could, for instance expose an API that returns a, b, c directly.
In most uses a, c would be zero.

Ok, do you have ‘netconf agent ssh’ configured?
And I assume you have ‘netconf-yang agent ssh’ also configured.

‘ssh server netconf port 1500’ is to enable netconf subsystem for netconf-yang agent with port 1500.
To enable subsystem for netconf-xml agent, you need ‘ssh server capability netconf-xml’ configured.
Since netconf-yang agent works fine with ncclient, you did not have an issue when using port 1500 because you connect to netconf-yang agent.

To summarize;


  1.  This enables netconf-xml agent on port 22
  netconf agent tty
                   !
   ssh server capability netconf-xml


  1.  This enables netconf-yang agent on the specified port (830 if port is not specified)
   netconf-yang agent ssh
  !
   ssh server netconf port <port#>


Thanks,
Norio

```
def ctc_loss(y_pred, y_true, eos_token=0, sequence_length=320):
    with tf.name_scope("CTCLoss"):
        #TODO: I've created a dense_to_sparse op in tensorflow so this should be replaced once it's released.
        indices = tf.where(tf.not_equal(y_true, tf.constant(eos_token, dtype=y_true.dtype)))
        values = tf.gather_nd(y_true, indices)
        shape = tf.shape(y_true, out_type=tf.int64)
        sparse_y_true = tf.SparseTensor(
            indices,
            values,
            shape
        )
        return tf.nn.ctc_loss(labels=sparse_y_true, inputs=y_pred, sequence_length=sequence_length)

No, this is usually overkill. For large datasets, the usual pattern is to divide them into three pieces:
- train: for direct model optimization
- tune: for hyper-parameter optimization
- evaluate: for evaluating final models

We also discussed how best to combine this with hyperparameter search metaestimators.  There is a tradeoff between combining multiple incremental pass computations (which share I/O nicely) and not computing too many (so that we can get a first result quickly).  When there is more work than cores it's not clear how to make this tradeoff well.  

This may be more of a research question and thus beyond our scope starting out, but is something to keep in mind.

Well, TRiP98 ints are saved on 2 bytes, this means maximum possible number is order of 32000. 
Adding two cubes most probably won't be a problem, but multiplication might be more tricky.

Even with low numbers you may get overflow: 200*200 = 40000.
It means when cube `d` is saved as `int16` then calculating `d*d` will likely result in an overflow.

In this situation (`d * d` + int overflow) we may:
 - print an warning and upcast to `int32`
 - print an error and rise an exection, tell user to manually do casting ( `d.astype(np.float32)` or `d.astype(np.int32)`)

First option seems natural at first glance, but imagine user doing:
`d*d / (d+1)`. The resulting cube will be `int32` and when saved as binary file which would be twice as large and probably incompatible with TRiP98.

On python side you have nice decimal type (https://docs.python.org/2/library/decimal.html) which can solve the issue, but it is not supported by numpy :/

Hey guys thanks for the useful input! I understand it would be quite a radical change for the parameters, and I must admit that I don't have a completely worked out idea. I just wanted to put this idea forth to see if it's a viable option or not.
While I agree with @adriaanrol that parameters work well in their current form, especially for drivers, I'm running into its limitations with more abstract classes unrelated to drivers.

To give a concrete example, we use pulse sequences that consists of `Pulse` objects. Each `Pulse` has attributes `t_start`, `t_stop`, and `duration`. Now `pulse.t_start` and `pulse.duration` are things we could want to vary in a measurement, so we want them to be parameters. `pulse.t_stop(=pulse.t_start + pulse.duration)` on the other hand is only there for convenience and is much easier to implement as a dependent property (`@property`). If we now want the center time of the pulse, it would be `(pulse.t_start() + pulse.t_stop) / 2`, i.e. we would have to remember that t_start is a parameter and t_stop is not. Of course we could also do `pulse.t_start() + pulse.duration()/2`, so this example has a loophole, but I have been runnning into similar situations quite often. The main point I'm trying to make here is that forcing every attribute of every object to be a parameter places quite a large contraint on objects, especially since many such attributes may never ever need the functionality that a parameter provides. Some classes don't need any parameters, but do interface with other classes that do, would these then also need all its attributes to be parameters, or do we need to remember which classes contain parameters and which ones do not? my sense is that having two types of attributes (standard attributes and parameters) that behave in different ways will start to cause issues the larger the framework around QCoDeS becomes.

The main proposal is therefore to change the parameter from being a container for a value to being (an extension to) the value itself. That we would need type-specific parameters is merely a consequence of this. From the outside, these parameter attributes behave just as the type it represents. @alexcjohnson this also solves one of the issue you raised, as you would simultaneously pass both the value and the parameter.

To be clear, the parameters would still have get/set functionality that can be called via brackets. Coincidentally, this should solve most of the major backwards compatibility issues, as parameters can still operate just as before. 
@WilliamHPNielsen My idea is to step away from descriptors, and instead use instrument.__setattr__ to implement `instrument.parameter = value`. This way, we can still define parameters for each class instance. @alexcjohnson you are right that `p = instrument.parameter; p = 5` would not work, but you can still do `p(5)`, and it should make sense that `p = 5` wouldn't update the parameter value, but create an integer p with value 5.

However, one important issue raised by @WilliamHPNielsen that I hadn't thought much about before is the difference between get and get_latest. Should calling `instrument.parameter` update its value by callling `instrument.parameter.get()`, or simply keep its latest value? The former might lead to issues that it calls a `.get` when it shouldn't, while the latter can lead to situations where `instrument.frequency` shows an old value that is not up to date. I'm not sure what the best solution is. I veer towards the latter, which is also easier to implement. In this case, a `.get` is only called during a Loop, and with `instrument.parameter()`. But perhaps there's a better solution I haven't thought of.

@WilliamHPNielsen Type-specific parameter still need validators to ensure that an IntParameter will have an integer value. These validators can still have additional constraints (e.g. multiples of 2)

I'm hoping this could serve as a bridge between having many attributes as parameters, while not having to worry if an attribute is a parameter or not. Parameters could still be used in the old way (except changing type). I understand the point brought up by @adriaanrol that too many parameter type might complicate things, though if their behavior is identical except for their type properties this should be manageable.The major issue I'm seeing is how to deal with get versus get_latest, would this be a deal breaker?

@Francesco149 

> player-passthrough=hls

By using this parameter, you're just passing through the resolved HLS master playlist URL to MPV and using it as its input parameter. MPV is then downloading the stream segments by itself instead of reading a concatenated stream from Streamlink, which means that both the `hls-segment-threads` and `hls-live-edge` parameters of Streamlink become pointless. It's basically the same as running
```bash
mpv --cache=no "$(streamlink --stream-url URL QUALITY)"
```
The other parameters you've listed are unrelated to viewing Twitch streams...

The reason why you're having a slightly lower delay by using this method is because you've disabled (minimized) MPV's network cache value and you're not using Streamlink's additional caching layer and its default configuration like when you're piping the concatenated stream to the player process:
https://mpv.io/manual/stable/#options-cache

However, if you properly set Streamlink's parameters, you'll see that there's almost no difference in streaming delay. It mostly just depends on the timing and availability of the HLS playlist/segments while launching the stream.
```bash
streamlink -p mpv -a '--cache=no {filename}' --hls-live-edge 1 --hls-segment-threads 2 URL QUALITY
```

You can compare it like this:
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
CHANNEL="name"
streamlink -p mpv -a '--cache=no --title "With Streamlink" {filename}' --hls-live-edge 1 --hls-segment-threads 2 "twitch.tv/${CHANNEL}" best & \
mpv --cache=no --title "Without Streamlink" "$(streamlink --stream-url "twitch.tv/${CHANNEL}" best)"
```

Since MPV doesn't support Twitch's custom HLS implementation, you won't be able to beat their web player for now, but if this gets implemented in Streamlink some day in the future, you should hopefully be able to see a lower delay than just using MPV.

@ejeschke -- I *think* I'm at the point now that I understand enough about how events work in the browser, what events are available, and how to get information about those events back and forth to python that I can put together something fairly general, in two sense:

+ The full list of events you [listed above] should be doable. In principle, much of the javascript code is the same for attaching listeners for each type of event,  and the event handler on the javascript side is pretty simple, just passing a message to the python side.
+ The mouse/keyboard event handlers should be attachable to any kind of widget (not just `Image`). I'm mostly interested in making `Image` work, but the code I'd add to make just image work would be virtually the same as the code needed to make the same handling work on any widget. The details of attaching the listeners is different somewhat if you are writing them for a single widget type (backbone handles the setup and teardown of listeners in that case) vs for the general case, but not really much easier or harder.

`Image` widgets need a little extra magic to get a useful (i.e. location in the original array) x,y for a mouse event because that translation from click x,y to array x,y depends on the dimensions (and padding, etc) of which *view* of the image you click and on the dimensions of the array.

A `Canvas` widget (which I'm not proposing to write at this point) allows for much more complicated transformations of the image on the javascript side (rotation and scaling), which would add another layer of complexity to getting the array x,y out.

My ETA for a draft of the implementation of a couple of event types (say click and dblclick) is a couple of days, planning to work up what it would look like in a couple arrangements:

+ As events added only to the `Image` widget.
+ As a separate flexible `MouseListener` widget that can be hooked up to a source widget (Image or Label or whatever) as suggested by @maartenbreddels. 
+ Adding a `listeners` property to the core DOMWidget (i.e. to all widgets) to which one can attach a `MouseListener`.

I understand that there's a lot in this pr.  I usually try to make things smaller, but there was just too much interconnected in this one.  The tests added a lot as well.

There were two primary goals for these changes that these changes needed to meet to allow using localstack in our application's development/testing:

1) Ability to specify the docker network -- our environment runs in a specific docker-compose and uses python3 lambdas, so the docker containers launched by localstack for the lambdas need to be able to talk to the other services (including localstack) that are running in the docker compose stack.

2) Ability to view logs from lambdas.  This is not unique to our environment, and seems to be a big piece that was missing from localstack for lambda development.  While synchronous lambda calls can have their output logged to the localstack docker log,  logs from async logs are lost.   This change allows us to set the logging options for the docker containers as they are launched by localstack.  In my case, I have an syslog server running which collects the logs and places the output into their own file based on the name of the lambda.

Thanks,

In the future, a pull request is the way to go [using a branch]. They're pretty easy and means I can just click one button to merge ;)

E.g:
1. `git remote add -f upstream git://github.com/lincolnloop/python-qrcode.git` (`-f` saves us a `git fetch upstream`)
2. `git checkout upstream/master -b tty-fix`
3. Make changes then `git commit -am "Make print_tty work on both black and white backgrounds. Fixes #4"`
4. `git push origin tty-fix`
5. Go to github, switch to the tty-fix branch, click the pull request button.
